turns.content: I've read about the visitor pattern before but I still don't fully grasp how it fits into Rust's type system compared to other languages. Can you explain why we use enums or traits here instead of just adding methods directly?

In Rust, the visitor pattern is typically implemented using enum-based dispatch for performance and safety when dealing with static type structures like an AST. Instead of relying on dynamic polymorphism which would require a trait object like &dyn Visitor, you define your node types to implement a specific trait or simply use pattern matching on an enum that represents all possible node variants. This allows the compiler to generate specialized code paths for each variant rather than dispatching through a virtual table.
That makes sense for performance, but what about when I need more flexibility or runtime behavior? Should I use trait-based visitors instead?

Trait-based visitors are useful when your node hierarchy is unstable or you want to introduce new node types without recompiling the visitor logic. You would define a generic Visitor trait with an accept method that takes a Node reference, and then implement this trait for different visitor implementations. However, be aware that trait objects in Rust have some overhead due to vtable lookup, so enum-based dispatch remains preferred when your AST structure is known at compile time.
I see the difference now, but could you give me a concrete example of how this looks with an actual node type and visitor implementation?

Sure, imagine you have an Expr enum containing BinOp, UnOp, and Lit variants. You would define a Visitor trait with an accept method that takes &Self and &Expr. Then you'd implement this trait for a specific visitor like a printer or analyzer. The key is that your visitor implementation contains the logic for each node type via pattern matching on the Expr enum, effectively doing double dispatch where the compiler knows exactly which branch to take based on the concrete expression type.

I'm stuck on my AST walker for this weekend project and I think I'm doing it wrong. I tried implementing a trait-based visitor but the compiler keeps complaining about orphan rules when I try to add methods to the node types without changing their existing impl blocks.

The issue is likely that you're trying to add generic methods to non-generic structs or using traits where they aren't allowed in Rust. The better approach for this scenario is the enum-based visitor pattern which leverages double dispatch through an enum rather than a trait object. You can define a Visitor enum with variants matching each node type and then use match statements on that enum instead of relying on trait objects.
Right, so I should just create a separate enum for my visitor? But how do I avoid duplicating code across all the different node types when I have things like BinaryExpr, UnaryOp, and FunctionDef?

You don't duplicate code by implementing methods on each node type. Instead, you implement a single walk method on your main AST struct that takes a visitor enum as an argument. The double dispatch happens implicitly because the compiler knows exactly which variant of the visitor to call based on the current node type when you iterate through the tree. This is much cleaner than trying to make every node trait-implementing.

Okay, so I have my AST struct and now I need to build the visitor enum with all those specific node variants like BinaryExprVisitor or UnaryOpVisitor? And then how do I actually walk down the tree using this?

Exactly. You define a Visitor enum where each variant corresponds to a specific AST node type, then you implement methods on that enum like accept() for each visitor variant. The walk method on your main struct calls the appropriate visitor variant based on which node it currently holds, effectively creating double dispatch without needing any trait objects or orphan rules complications.
That sounds promising but I'm worried about performance since Rust sometimes gets weird with this kind of pattern. Will this be as fast as my original trait-based approach?

The enum-based visitor is actually faster in practice because it avoids the virtual dispatch overhead that traits introduce and eliminates the need for vtables. Since you're walking a known structure like an AST where every node type is fixed, the compiler can optimize this into very efficient code without any runtime polymorphism cost.
Great, so I should just replace my trait-based visitor with this enum approach? Any specific crates or patterns I should be aware of for this kind of thing?

You don't need external crates for the pattern itself since it's fundamental to Rust. The standard library doesn't provide a built-in visitor system, so you'll implement your own using just basic enums and structs. Just keep in mind that if you ever need to add new node types later, you only need to update one place: your walk method.
Perfect, I think I understand now. One last question before I start coding - what's the best way to handle state accumulation during this traversal like counting or aggregating values?

Use a separate struct that holds your accumulated state and pass it through every visitor variant in your enum. Each visit method will update that struct, so by the time you finish walking the entire tree, you'll have all your counts and aggregates ready without needing to store intermediate results.

I'm trying to port my legacy parser from Python to Rust, and I keep running into the problem of handling different node types in an AST. How do I avoid writing a separate if-else chain for every single operation?

That's a classic pain point when porting dynamic languages to statically typed ones. The Python way uses duck typing, but Rust needs explicit contracts. Think of it like a vending machine: instead of guessing which slot your coin belongs to based on shape (dynamic), you have a specific chip that tells the machine exactly what kind of item to dispense (static). In Rust's AST walking, this translates to using either an enum-based visitor or a trait-based one to ensure every node has a defined behavior.
I guess I thought I could just use standard Rust enums and write a function that takes the node type and returns a result. But then how do I actually dispatch to the right handler without extra code?

Ah, you're thinking about a simple enum match, but that won't work for generic operations easily unless you use a trait. The Rust way is to define a Trait called Visitor that requires an impl for each node type. This acts like a contract ensuring every AST node knows how to handle the visit operation. It's similar to how your Python code might have methods on classes, but here the behavior is enforced at compile time so you never miss a case.
Okay, so I define a trait and then implement it for every node type? That sounds like double dispatch. Can you give me an example of how the enum visitor approach looks in Rust code?

Sure, imagine your AST has Node, BinOp, and UnaryOp types. You create a Visitor trait with methods named binop_visit and unary_visit. Then you implement that trait for each type so the compiler forces double dispatch logic. The enum approach is actually cleaner because it lets you use pattern matching to switch on the node type without needing an extra visitor object. It's like having a switchboard where pressing a button directly routes the call instead of using an intermediary relay.
I see, so I can just match on the enum variant inside my function and handle each case? But what if I want to reuse code across multiple operations or different AST types?

That's where the trait-based approach shines. If you need to run many different visitors over your AST, having a common Trait lets you write generic functions that accept any visitor type. You can pass a custom Visitor instance or reuse a standard one without duplicating logic. It's like having a universal adapter for all your node types so you don't have to rewrite the same handler multiple times.
Hmm, I'm still confused about which is better. Should I use the enum pattern matching or the trait approach? My AST grows as I add more operator types.

If your AST stays small and you only need one visitor at a time, the enum match is simpler and faster since there's no indirection. But if you're adding new operators frequently or want to swap out visitors dynamically, then the trait approach scales better. Think of it like choosing between hardwiring wires for each device versus using a universal power strip that can plug in any appliance later.

Got it, so I should probably go with the trait approach since my project is expected to evolve. But how do I make sure the compiler catches errors if I forget to implement the trait for a new node type?

Perfect question. The Rust compiler will scream at you immediately if you add a new enum variant without implementing the corresponding Trait method. This is one of the biggest benefits: zero runtime surprises because missing an implementation is a hard compile-time error. You can't run code that violates this contract, so your AST always remains consistent and safe.
That's reassuring! One last thing, how do I handle the case where I want to perform different operations on the same node type depending on some context?

You can extend your Trait with additional methods or create a wrapper struct that holds both the visitor and extra state. Alternatively, you might use a combination of traits where one trait handles base operations and another adds specialized behavior. It's like having a vehicle chassis that supports different engines—you keep the core structure stable while adding flexibility through composition.

I'm trying to parse a custom domain-specific language where the grammar has nested expressions like arithmetic and logical operations. I want to walk through this abstract syntax tree without writing separate `match` branches for every possible node type, so I can keep my code DRY while walking the tree.

That is exactly why Rust developers choose the Visitor pattern over a simple match expression when dealing with complex ASTs. Instead of duplicating logic in multiple `if let Some(Node::Add(...))` branches, you define a trait called `Visitable` and implement it for each node type like `Node::BinOp`, `Node::UnaryMinus`, or `Node::Literal`. You then create a visitor struct that holds the state of your walk. When you call `visit()` on the root node, Rust's type system ensures double dispatch happens automatically because the trait object method is bound to the concrete implementation at compile time.
So if I have an addition node and a subtraction node, they both implement the same trait? And how do I avoid the overhead of `dyn Trait` since that usually implies heap allocation?

You are correct about the trait implementation, but you don't necessarily need to use `dyn Trait`. If your AST nodes are all part of a single enum variant group like `enum Expr { Add(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>), Sub(...) }`, Rust allows you to implement the trait directly on the enum itself using an associated function or by implementing it per variant without boxing them all. You can then pass the visitor as a generic parameter `<T: Visitor>` to your walking function, which avoids the indirection of `dyn Trait` while still leveraging compile-time type safety instead of runtime polymorphism.
I'm concerned about performance when the tree is large because I worry about virtual dispatch overhead. Is there a way to make this zero-cost in Rust?

Yes, you can achieve zero-cost double dispatch by using an enum-based visitor pattern where each variant of your AST enum explicitly matches against its corresponding visitor method rather than using a trait object at runtime. Instead of calling `visit(node)` with a trait object, you match on the concrete node type and call the specific implementation like `node.visit(visitor)`. This eliminates any virtual dispatch overhead because the compiler generates direct function calls for each path in your enum, making the performance identical to hand-written recursion.
That sounds promising. Can I use a crate like `enum_dispatch` or just write this manually with macros?

You can absolutely write this manually without any crates if you want full control over the implementation details, and it is often preferable for performance-critical parsers. You can define your AST node types as separate structs or an enum and implement a trait called `Accept` on each one. Then you create a macro that generates the dispatch logic for you based on the list of node types you defined, which reduces boilerplate significantly while keeping the code transparent enough to debug if something goes wrong.
I think I understand how the pattern works now but I'm still curious about why this is better than just using `match` statements for every possible operator in my language?

The key advantage here is that the visitor logic becomes a single cohesive unit rather than scattered across hundreds of match branches. If you add a new operator to your grammar later, you only need to implement its `Accept` method and update your main walker loop; you don't have to modify existing code that handles other operators like multiplication or assignment. This encapsulation makes the parser much more maintainable because changes are localized to specific node types instead of requiring a global refactor.
Okay, so I can implement this for my custom parser now and avoid the spaghetti code from all those match statements?

Exactly, you will find that structuring your AST with traits and a dedicated visitor class makes the parsing logic much cleaner. You can keep your main loop simple by just iterating over the tree nodes and calling their respective visit methods without worrying about branching logic for every single operator type.

I'm studying for my Rust interview and I keep hearing about the visitor pattern. Does this just mean using an enum to match different node types in an AST like I do with pattern matching on expressions?

That's a great starting point, but there's a key distinction you need to make between simple pattern matching and the visitor pattern. While Rust enums are perfect for distinguishing node types in an AST, the visitor pattern is about how those nodes interact with external logic. Instead of writing one giant if-else chain or using match on every operation, you define a trait that each node type implements, like NodeVisitor, which allows you to walk the tree dynamically without knowing the exact structure beforehand.
So I would implement traits for each expression type in my parser? Like a trait for BinaryOp and one for UnaryOp?

Exactly, but you don't necessarily need separate traits for every single operator. You can use a common base trait like ExprVisitor that all expression types implement, then specialize the methods or use generics to handle different operations on the same interface. This keeps your code DRY and makes it easier to add new operators later without touching the existing tree structure.
I'm getting confused between this and double dispatch. Is double dispatch just a benefit of having these traits implemented?

You've hit on the core concept here. Double dispatch in Rust is exactly what happens when you use traits for AST walking because the compiler generates different code based on both the visitor's type and the node's type at compile time, rather than runtime checks. This means your tree traversal logic stays fast and predictable, avoiding any overhead from virtual method calls or dynamic dispatch.
That sounds efficient but how does this compare to C++ where I had to worry about vtables?

The main advantage is that Rust's double dispatch happens at compile time, so you get the performance of a single dispatch with the flexibility of multiple types. You avoid the runtime cost and potential bugs associated with C++ vtables entirely while still being able to extend your AST easily.
I'm getting this error when trying to walk my AST: 'cannot find a method named dispatch on the enum Result'. I tried using trait objects but it's failing in release mode. What went wrong?

The issue is that you're likely relying on dynamic dispatch through trait objects which Rust doesn't support for non-trait-object types without explicit handling, and even then it has performance penalties. For your AST walking scenario where you need to visit different node types like NodeExpr and NodeBlock, you should use the enum-based visitor pattern instead of trait objects. This allows compile-time double dispatch so each variant gets its own implementation of the Visit trait without runtime overhead.
So I need to implement a custom Visitor trait for my nodes and then use it on the enum Result? But how do I handle the error cases properly?

Yes, you define your NodeVisitor trait with methods like visit_expr and visit_block that accept specific node types. Then implement this trait for each node variant in your enum Result - something like impl<T: std::fmt::Debug> NodeResult<T> { fn dispatch(&self, visitor: &mut T) -> bool } where the implementation pattern matches exactly which variant you're visiting. For error cases, simply return false from that specific visit method or propagate errors through the chain using Result types in your visitor methods.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about how to structure the data flow between my parser and the walker. Should I separate them entirely?

You should absolutely separate them - your parser produces a Result<Node, ParseError> while your visitor consumes it. Think of this as two distinct phases where the first builds the AST and the second performs operations on it using the double dispatch mechanism you're implementing through your enum-based visitor pattern.
I'm worried about performance since I heard dynamic dispatch can be slow. Is there a way to keep things fast while still being generic?

The key is avoiding trait objects entirely - by using enum variants with their own implementations of the same trait, you get zero-cost abstraction through monomorphization. Rust's compiler will generate separate code paths for each concrete node type in your Result enum, giving you the flexibility without the runtime cost that comes from dynamic dispatch.
Wait, but what if I want to extend this later with new node types? Won't that break my existing visitor implementations?

Actually, you can make your design more robust by extracting the common logic into a generic helper function or trait. This way when you add new variants like NodeFunction to your Result enum, you only need to implement the visitor for that specific variant while reusing existing implementations for other types through composition rather than breaking everything.
Could I use crates like this to speed up my implementation? Or should I just write it manually?

You can definitely use crates like syn for parsing and derive macros from serde or similar libraries to generate boilerplate code, but the core visitor logic itself - that double dispatch pattern on your enum Result - should be written manually. These crates help with syntax tree generation but won't magically solve the architectural decision about how you structure your dispatch mechanism.
I think I understand now. One more thing though - what happens if my parser returns an error? Does that stop everything?

The visitor should check the result type before proceeding - if your parse function returns Err, you simply don't call the walk method at all. The dispatch logic only runs on successfully parsed nodes, so error handling stays isolated to the parsing phase while keeping your visitor pattern clean and focused on successful AST traversal.
